title: Sliding-Window Moving Average

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Array A of length m, window size w. Compute B[i] = (A[i-w+1] + ... + A[i]) / w for i >= w.
rules: Naive: recompute the sum from scratch at each window. Theta(m * w).
Sliding sum: at each step, S = S - A[i-w] + A[i]. Theta(m).
Each element enters and leaves the window exactly once.
setup.question: What is the optimal time complexity?

learningObjectives: Recognize the sliding-window pattern: O(1) amortized update.
Avoid recomputing what was already computed.
Generalize to other window aggregates (max via deque, variance via running sums of squares).

stages.label: Sliding update rule
stages.prompt: Initial S = A[1] + ... + A[w]. To advance the window from index i-1 to i, what's the update?

options.text: S = S + A[i] (just add the new).
S = S - A[i] (just remove the old).

stages.explanation: Subtract the element that LEAVES the window (A[i-w]) and add the element that ENTERS (A[i]). One add and one subtract per step.

stages.label: Commit
stages.template: Naive moving average: __[a]__ time.  Sliding window: __[b]__ time.  Sliding works because each element is added/subtracted exactly __[c]__ time(s).
stages.explanation: Naive: m windows * w terms each = Theta(mw). Sliding: each element joins and leaves the window exactly once -> Theta(m). Order-of-magnitude speedup for large w.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: How would you compute a rolling MAX in O(m)? (Use a monotonic deque -- elements pop from the back when a new larger element arrives.)
How does this relate to compounded financial returns? (Same prefix-sum pattern works on log returns.)
Why is floating-point sliding-sum unstable for very long arrays? (Cumulative round-off error; use Kahan summation or block resets.)
